Copula calibration. 2014. 13A Expressing Popular Forms of Calibration as Distribution Matching A.1 Calibration in Regression Here, we show how the forms of calibration listed in Table 1 can be expressed in terms of conditional distribution matching. Note that many of these forms of calibration are sensible in the classification setting as well (e.g., marginal, group, and decision calibration). Quantile Calibration Let Y be a continuous random variable. A forecaster is quantile calibrated if P � QY |X(Y ) ≤ t  = t, ∀t ∈ [0, 1]. (6) This is equivalent to stating that QY |X(Y ) is uniform on the interval [0, 1]. For continuous random variables, the probability integral transform PY |X(Y ) is uniform on the interval [0, 1]. Thus, quantile calibration can be written as QY |X(Y ) ≜ PY |X(Y ), where ≜ denotes equality in distribution. Threshold Calibration Let Y be a continuous random variable. A forecaster satisfies threshold calibration [39] if P(QY |X(Y ) ≤ t | QY |X(y) ≤ c) = t for all t ∈ [0, 1], c ∈ [0, 1], and y ∈ Y. This states that QY |X(Y ) is uniform, conditioned on the event QY |X(y) ≤ c. Noting that PY |X(Y ) is uniform, the constraint is then equivalent to the following: QY |X(Y ) ≜ PY |X(Y ) | 1   QY |X(y) ≤ c 	 , ∀c ∈ [0, 1], y∈ Y (7) Marginal Calibration A forecaster is marginally calibrated [11] if E[QY |X(y)] = PY (y) for all y ∈ Y. Recall that the distribution of bY is defined by (bY | X = x) ∼ QY |x. Thus, the marginal distribution of bY is given by bY ∼ E[QY |X]. So marginal calibration states that bY and Y have the same marginal cdf or, equivalently, bY ≜ Y . Decision Calibration Zhao et al. [52] introduce the concept of LK decision calibration for multi- class classification, where Y = {0, 1}C is the set of onehot vectors over C classes. Definition A.1 (LK-Decision Calibration, Zhao et al. [52]). Let LK be the set of all loss functions with K actions LK = {ℓ : Y × A →R, |A| = K}, we say that a prediction QY |X is LK-decision calibrated (with respect to PY |X) if ∀ℓ ∈ LK and δ ∈ ∆LK EXEbY ∼QY |X [ℓ(bY , δ(QY |X))] = EXEY ∼PY |X [ℓ(Y, δ(QY |X))] (8) where ∆LK is the set of all Bayes decision rules over loss functions with K actions. They show that LK decision calibration can equivalently be expressed as requiring that E[(bY − Y )1{δ(QY |X) = a}] = 0 for all δ ∈ ∆LK . We can rewrite this as E[bY |δ(QY |X) = a] = E[Y |δ(QY |X) = a], ∀δ ∈ ∆LK , a∈ A (9) note that E[Y |δ(QY |X) = a] gives the conditional distribution of (Y | δ(QY |X) = a) due to the onehot representation of Y . Thus, it is equivalent to require that  bY ≜ Y  | δ(QY |X), ∀δ ∈ ∆LK (10) where ≜ denotes equality in distribution. Group Calibration Group calibration [20] says that forecasts are marginally calibrated for each subgroup (e.g., subgroups defined by gender or age). We can write this as bY ≜ Y | Group(X), where Group(X) is a variable indicating which subgroup includes the feature vector X. Distribution Calibration A forecaster satisfies distribution calibration [ 42] if for all possible forecasts Q0 and labels y ∈ Y, we have P � Y ≤ y | QY |X = Q0  = Q0(y). This is equivalent to requiring PY |X(y) | QY |X = QY |X(y) for all y ∈ Y. Since the cdfs take the same value at all y ∈ Y, the random variables Y and bY have the same distribution, giving us that bY ≜ Y | QY |X. 14Individual Calibration Let Y be a real-valued continuous random variable. Individual calibration [51] states that P � QY |x(Y ) ≤ t  = t, for all t ∈ [0, 1] and x ∈ X. Letting U ∼ Unif(0, 1), we can write this as QY |X(Y ) ≜ PY |X(Y ) | X (11) Q−1 Y |X(QY |X(Y )) ≜ Q−1 Y |X(PY |X(Y )) | X (12) Y ≜ Q−1 Y |X(U) | X (13) Y ≜ bY | X (14) Local Calibration A forecaster is locally calibrated [27] if QY |X and PY |X are equal, on average, in any neighborhood of the feature space. Similarity in feature space is defined by a kernel k(x, x′). In regression, local calibration for quantiles says that E[1   QY |X(Y ) ≤ t 	 k(X, x)] E[k(X, x)] = t, ∀t ∈ [0, 1], x∈ X. (15) This can equivalently be stated as E[1   QY |X(Y ) ≤ t 	 k(X, x)] = E[1   PY |X(Y ) ≤ t 	 k(X, x)], ∀t ∈ [0, 1], x∈ X (16) or, in terms of the canonical feature mapping, E[1   QY |X(Y ) ≤ t 	 ⟨ϕ(X), ϕ(x)⟩] = E[1   PY |X(Y ) ≤ t 	 ⟨ϕ(X), ϕ(x)⟩], ∀t ∈ [0, 1], x∈ X. (17) Thus, it is sufficient for E[QY |X] and E[PY |X] to have the same distribution conditioned on the feature mapping ϕ(X). This can be written succinctly as Y ≜ bY | ϕ(X) (18) A.2 Calibration in Classification Here, we express three popular forms of calibration from the classification literature—namely, canonical, top-label, and marginal calibration [ 44, Eq (1-3)]—in terms of distribution matching constraints. For the entirety of this section, Y is a discrete random variable taking values in the set Y = {1, 2, . . . , m}, and qY |x is the probability mass function of the forecast given the features X = x. Canonical Calibration A forecaster satisfies canonical calibration ifP � Y = y | qY |X  = qY |X(y) for all y ∈ Y. This says that the label follows the same distribution under the forecast and the true distribution, conditioned on qY |X. In other words, Y ≜ bY | qY |X. Top-Label Calibration A forecaster satisfies top-label calibration if P � Y = Y ∗ | qY |X(Y ∗)  = qY |X(Y ∗), where Y ∗ := arg maxy∈Y qY |X(y) is the mode of the forecast. This equation can be rewritten in terms of the indicators E  1 {Y = Y ∗} |qY |X(Y ∗)  = E h 1{bY = Y ∗} |qY |X(Y ∗) i and the indicator variables are equal in expectation if and only if they are equal in distribution. Thus, top-label calibration is equivalent to 1 {Y = Y ∗} ≜ 1{bY = Y ∗} |qY |X(Y ∗). Marginal Calibration A forecaster satisfies marginal calibration if P � Y = y | qY |X(y)  = qY |X(y) for all y ∈ Y. Similar to top-label calibration, this can be written as E  1 {Y = y} |qY |X(y)  = E h 1{bY = y} |qY |X(y) i and the indicator variables are equal in expectation if and only if they are equal in distribution. Thus, marginal calibration can be written as 1 {Y = y} ≜ 1{bY = y} |qY |X(y), for all y ∈ Y. 15B Experimental Setup and Additional Results B.1 Reproducibility In order to reproduce the experiments and results obtained in this paper, we provide details about the hyperparameter tuning and required computational resources. Hyperparameter Search: We use TPES [ 3] to perform hyperparameter optimization. For all experiments, we vary: • Layer sizes between 32 and 512 • RBF kernel bandwidths between 0.001 and 200 • Batch sizes between 16 and 512, with and without batch normalization • Learning rates between 10−7 and 10−1. • The loss mixture weight λ (as in NLL +λ · MMD and XE +λ · MMD) between 0.1 and 1000. For each dataset, we test 100 hyperparameter settings for each training objective for regression {NLL, NLL + MMD}, and classification setting {XE, XE + MMCE, XE + KDE, XE + MMD}. For each run, we enable early-stopping if the validation loss does not improve for more than 50 epochs. In the regression setting, we pick the best performing run based on the validation NLL. For classification, we select the best performing run based on the validation accuracy, with validation ECE used for break ties. For each model and dataset, the best performing model is then re-run with 50 random seeds to gather information about standard errors and statistical significance. To capture variability across different seeds, we report the standard error of the mean in Tables 3, 4, and 5. Kernel Bandwidth We select the RBF kernel bandwidth for training on each dataset using the aforementioned hyperparameter optimization. For each dataset in Tables 4 and 5, the KDE metric we report is for the bandwidth selected through a held-out validation set, scaled so that the error is of a comparable order of magnitude across datasets (since multiplying a kernel by a positive scalar preserves the kernel properties). Baselines In the classification setting, we compare our approach to two state-of-the-art trainable calibration methods, MMCE [26] and KDE [37]. For MMCE, we follow the same experimental setup as the official author implementation, where the loss mixture weight λ and the Laplacian kernel bandwidth are chosen via a held-out validation set. For KDE, we use the L1 canonical calibration using the ECE KDE estimator, calculated according to Equation (9) from Popordanoska et al. [37]. We follow the same experimental setup as the official author implementation, where the kernel bandwidth is chosen using leave-one-out-likelihood (LOO MLE), and the loss mixture weight λ is chosen via a held-out validation set. Computational Requirements: All experiments were conducted on a single CPU machine (Intel(R) Xeon(R) Gold 6342 CPU @ 2.80GHz), utilizing 8 cores per experiment. When run on a CPU, the training time for a single model ranges from 5 to 15 minutes, depending on the dataset size. It is possible to perform a full hyperparameter sweep using this setup in ∼24 hours. To accelerate training, we have also run some experiments using an 11GB NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti. When run on a GPU, the training time for a single model ranges from 2 to 5 minutes, depending on the dataset size, and a hyperparameter sweep takes ∼10 hours. US Agriculture Dataset: We introduce a new CROP -YIELD dataset for predicting yearly wheat crop yield from geospatial weather data for different counties across the US. We use NOAA database which contains weather data from thousands of stations across the United States. For each county, we track the weather sequence of each year into a few summary statistics for each month (aver- age/maximum/minimum temperatures, precipitation, cooling/heating degree days). To study how climate change and location affect crop yield, we match crop yield data from the NASS dataset to weather stations in the NOAA database, and learn a function to predict crop yield from weather data. We specifically considered the crop yield of wheat across different counties in the US between 1990-2007. Since crop yield data for 1995 is the least sparse, we hold out data from 1995 for visualization, and train our forecasters on the remaining data. 16B.2 Additional Experimental Results Table 5: Performance on classification tasks, comparing MMCE regularization [26], KDE regulariza- tion [37], and MMD regularization (Ours), alongside a standard cross-entropy (XE) loss. Concretely, the decision loss ℓ : A × Y →R, for an action a ∈ {−1, +1} and a label y ∈ Y, is defined as ℓ(a, y) = 1 {a ̸= sign(y − c)}. Setup In order to achieve decision calibration, we tailor our kernels kX, kY to closely follow the decision problem. More concretely, we choose a kernel function that penalizes predicted labels which are on the wrong side of c, namely kY (y, y′) = tanh( y − c) · tanh(y′ − c). Notice that kY (y, y′) ≈ +1 when sign(y − c) = sign(y − c), and −1 otherwise. Thus, the calibration metric gives the model training feedback that is well aligned with the decision problem. To encourage decision calibration across all features, we set kX to be the universal RBF kernel. As part of this experiment, we investigate whether tailoring an MMD kernel to the decision problem will improve decision calibration over using a universal RBF kernel for both kX, kY . Metric To evaluate the effectiveness of methods, we compute the standard Decision Calibration Error (DCE) across all regression datasets, defined as DCE2 = X a∈A        EP [ℓ(Y, a)] − EXEbY ∼QY |X [ℓ(bY , a)]         2 2 , (19) where P is the true distribution over X × Y, and QY |X is a forecaster. Additional Results: Choice of kernel We present two sets of results. The first set of results is shown in Table 7, comparing models trained using our objective (NLL + MMD), where one uses a kernel kY that is tailored to the decision problem (tanh), while the other uses a universal RBF kernel. As described in Section 6.3, we observe that using the tanh kernel clearly achieves better decision calibration across all dataset, with minimal degradation in sharpness, i.e. NLL. This demonstrates that we can improve decision calibration using our trainable calibration metrics by tailoring our kernels to closely follow the decision problem. The second set of results, shown in Figure 2, further demonstrates the relationship between our trainable calibration metrics and decision calibration throughout model training. By tailoring our kernels to closely follow the decision problem, we observe that decision calibration is optimized throughout training. In contrast, using objectives that are agnostic to the decision problem (i.e. NLL, or MMD with RBF kernels) will lead to noticeably worse decision calibration, with little to no improvement throughout training. The same trend is observed on a held-out validation set, as shown in Figure 3. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces a unified, trainable framework that casts many calibration notions as conditional distribution matching and measures them with MMD. Provides unbiased, differentiable calibration metrics usable as regularizers alongside proper scoring rules to improve calibration without sacrificing sharpness. Shows how to tailor kernels to downstream decision problems to ensure accurate loss estimation and no-regret decisions, including extensions to infinite action/loss families. Empirically demonstrates improved calibration, sharpness, and decision-making over post-hoc-only methods across regression and classification tasks, and improves local calibration; unifies 11 existing calibration notions across regression and classification.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Formulate calibration as an Integral Probability Metric between (Y,Z) pairs under true and forecast-induced distributions, using Maximum Mean Discrepancy with RKHS kernels. Choose conditioning variable Z to recover different calibration types (e.g., Z=X for individual/local, Z=Q_{Y|X} for distribution/decision). Use product kernels k((y,z),(y',z'))=k_Y(y,y')k_Z(z,z'), with universal kernels to guarantee equivalence when needed. Provide unbiased plug-in U-statistic MMD estimator; reduce variance by analytically marginalizing forecast randomness in classification or by multiple samples in regression. Optimize joint objective: min_θ Σ_i −log q_θ(y_i|x_i) + λ·MMD^2 on mini-batches, using differentiable sampling/reparameterization (e.g., Gaussian; Gumbel-Softmax for mixtures). Tailor k_Y to decision tasks (e.g., tanh-sign kernel for threshold decisions; moment features for squared-loss point estimates), and add universal k_Z for conditional guarantees. For quantile/threshold calibration, use kernels over [0,1] on probability integral transforms. Classification MMD is differentiable in class probabilities with O(n^2 m^2) complexity.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Datasets: Regression—UCI SUPERCONDUCTIVITY, CRIME, BLOG, FB-COMMENT, MEDICAL-EXPENDITURE; Classification—UCI BREAST-CANCER, HEART-DISEASE, ONLINE-SHOPPERS, DRY-BEAN, ADULT; plus CROP-YIELD (weather→wheat yield) for local calibration. Splits: 70% train / 10% validation / 20% test. Models: 3-layer feed-forward nets; regression outputs Gaussian (μ, σ^2); classification outputs logits. Training objectives: NLL or XE baselines; regularizers—MMCE, KDE (ECE-KDE), and proposed MMD. Post-hoc: regression isotonic quantile calibration; classification temperature scaling. Metrics: Regression—test NLL, Quantile Calibration Error (QCE), Decision Calibration Error (DCE), Kernel Calibration Error (KCE with RBF on X×Y); Classification—accuracy, ECE, average Shannon entropy (sharpness), KCE. Repetitions: 50 random seeds; report mean±SE. Hyperparameters via TPE search (layer sizes, RBF bandwidths, batch sizes, learning rate, λ); early stopping; model selection by val NLL (regression) or val accuracy with ECE tie-break (classification). Additional studies: number of forecast samples per example for MMD variance; tailored tanh kernel for decision calibration; Local Calibration Error (LCE) visualization on CROP-YIELD using geospatial kernels. Compute: CPU/GPU; MMD adds ~1.2–1.3× epoch time.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Requires differentiable sampling from the forecast distribution (limits applicable forecasters; relaxations needed). Regularization offers no finite-sample out-of-sample calibration guarantees by itself; performance depends on optimization and data. MMD scale and sensitivity depend on kernel choices/bandwidths, making absolute interpretability difficult. Universal kernels may need many samples and can be computationally heavy; classification MMD is O(n^2 m^2). Optimization can be challenging when conditioning variable Z is high-dimensional/expressive. Zero calibration error may be unattainable due to computational/data limits; added training cost over NLL/XE baselines.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Develop gradient estimators avoiding differentiable sampling (e.g., REINFORCE or control variates) and study variance–bias trade-offs. Scalable MMD approximations (random features, block/linear-time estimators, mini-batch U-statistics) and large-scale kernel learning or meta-learned kernels. Automatic kernel/bandwidth selection and task-aware kernel design with theoretical guarantees. Hybrid training with conformal or multicalibration methods to provide finite-sample guarantees while preserving sharpness; derive generalization bounds for calibration under MMD regularization. Extend to structured outputs, time series, and sequential decision-making; handle complex/infinite action spaces and families of losses with stronger conditional guarantees. Fairness-aware group/local calibration at scale; robustness to distribution shift and domain adaptation. Improved uncertainty parameterizations beyond Gaussian (mixtures, normalizing flows) with differentiable sampling. Online and continual calibration; adaptive resampling strategies to reduce MMD estimator variance.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_code:
# ===== Kernels and MMD/IPM losses (product-kernel MMD over (Y, Z)) =====
# File: src/metrics/losses.py
import torch
from torch import nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from typing import Optional, Dict
from src.utils import gmm_nll_dummy_x, gmm_params_to_dist

# Universal RKHS kernels (default RBF) and task kernels (e.g., tanh for threshold decisions)
def rbf_kernel(u: torch.Tensor, v: torch.Tensor, bandwidth=1):
    diff_norm_mat = torch.norm(u.unsqueeze(1) - v, dim=2).square()
    return torch.exp(- diff_norm_mat / bandwidth)

def tanh_kernel(u: torch.Tensor, v: torch.Tensor, bandwidth=1):
    out = torch.tanh(v) * torch.tanh(u).unsqueeze(1)  # handles Y via sign/tanh for threshold-type decisions
    return out.squeeze(2)

# Placeholders for other partition kernels if needed
def quadrant_partition_kernel(u: torch.Tensor, v: torch.Tensor):
    raise NotImplementedError()

def norm_partition_kernel(u: torch.Tensor, v: torch.Tensor):
    raise NotImplementedError()

kernel_funs = {
    "rbf": rbf_kernel,
    "partition_quadrant": quadrant_partition_kernel,
    "partition_norm": norm_partition_kernel,
    "tanh": tanh_kernel,
}

# Valid operands for constructing product kernels over (Y, Z)
# - 'y': response Y
# - 'x': features X (Z:=X for individual/local calibration)
# - 'p': probability integral transform U = F_theta(Y|X) (Z:=U for distributional calibration)
# - 'coords': optional spatial coords (example use-case)
VALID_OPERANDS = ['x', 'y', 'p', 'coords']


def mean_no_diag(A: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Unbiased U-statistic factor: average off-diagonal entries.
    Assumes square matrix A of size n x n.
    """
    assert A.dim() == 2 and A.shape[0] == A.shape[1]
    n = A.shape[0]
    A = A - torch.eye(n).to(A.device) * A.diag()
    return A.sum() / (n * (n - 1))


class GMMKernelLoss:
    """
    MMD loss for regression forecasters q_theta(y|x), with product kernels over selected operands.
    - operands: which variables to include and kernel type per variable (e.g., {'x':'rbf','y':'rbf'} or {'p':'rbf','x':'rbf'})
    - bandwidths: kernel parameters per operand
    - num_samples: number of Monte Carlo samples from q_theta to marginalize predictive randomness (variance reduction)
    - For op='y', uses rsample from torch distributions (i.e., reparameterized Gaussian/GMM)
    - For op='p', computes PIT U = F_theta(Y|X)
    """
    def __init__(
        self,
        operands: Dict[str, str] = {'x': "rbf", 'y': "rbf"},
        scalers: Optional[Dict] = None,
        num_samples: Optional[int] = 10,
        bandwidths: Optional[Dict] = {'x': 10.0, 'y': 0.1},
    ):
        assert all([op in VALID_OPERANDS for op in operands.keys()])
        if scalers is None:
            scalers = {op: 1. for op in operands.keys()}
        else:
            assert all(op in scalers for op in operands.keys())

        self.kernel_fun = {op: kernel_funs[kernel] for op, kernel in operands.items()}
        self.operands = list(operands.keys())
        self.scalers = scalers
        self.num_samples = num_samples
        self.bandwidths = bandwidths

    def __call__(self, x, y, preds, num_true: Optional[int] = None, use_dist=False, verbose=False):
        # Build a torch Distribution for q_theta
        if use_dist:
            dist = preds
        else:
            mean, std, prob = preds
            # For differentiable rsample of Y: require single Gaussian component; use Gumbel-Softmax if mixture
            if 'y' in self.operands:
                assert prob.shape[-1] == 1
            dist = gmm_params_to_dist(mean, std, prob)

        n = x.shape[0]
        if num_true is None:
            num_true = n
            true_idxs = torch.arange(0, n)
        else:
            # If sub-sampling true pairs is desired (note: unbiasedness details may change)
            true_idxs = torch.randint(low=0, high=n, size=(num_true,), device=x.device)

        loss_mats = [None, None, None]  # k(Z',Z') , k(Z',Z) , k(Z,Z)

        for op in self.operands:
            scaler = self.scalers[op]
            bandwidth = self.bandwidths[op]

            if op == 'coords':
                coords = x[:, :3]
                op_pred = coords
                op_true = coords[true_idxs]
                loss_mat  = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_pred, op_pred, bandwidth)
                loss_mat2 = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_pred, op_true, bandwidth)
                loss_mat3 = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_true, op_true, bandwidth)

            elif op == 'x':
                op_pred = x
                op_true = x[true_idxs]
                assert op_pred.dim() == 2 and op_true.dim() == 2
                loss_mat  = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_pred, op_pred, bandwidth)
                loss_mat2 = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_pred, op_true, bandwidth)
                loss_mat3 = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_true, op_true, bandwidth)

            elif op == 'y':
                y_pred = dist.rsample((self.num_samples,))  # S x N x D_y
                if y_pred.dim() == 2:
                    y_pred = y_pred.unsqueeze(-1)
                y_pred = y_pred.permute(1, 2, 0).to(x.device)  # N x D_y x S
                op_pred = y_pred
                op_true = y[true_idxs].view(-1, 1)

                # Monte Carlo average over predictive samples for MMD terms involving Y
                loss_mat  = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_pred, op_pred, bandwidth).mean(-1)
                loss_mat2 = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_pred, op_true.unsqueeze(-1), bandwidth).mean(-1)
                loss_mat3 = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_true, op_true, bandwidth)

            elif op == 'p':
                # Probability Integral Transform U = F_theta(Y|X)
                p_pred = dist.cdf(y).view(-1, 1)
                p_true = torch.rand(num_true, device=x.device).view(-1, 1)  # uniform reference
                op_pred = p_pred
                op_true = p_true
                loss_mat  = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_pred, op_pred, bandwidth)
                loss_mat2 = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_pred, op_true, bandwidth)
                loss_mat3 = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](op_true, op_true, bandwidth)

            if verbose:
                print(f'op={op}, op_pred.device={op_pred.device}, op_true.device={op_true.device}')

            for i, value in enumerate([loss_mat, loss_mat2, loss_mat3]):
                loss_mats[i] = value if loss_mats[i] is None else loss_mats[i] * value

        # Unbiased U-statistic MMD^2 over product kernel
        mmd2 = mean_no_diag(loss_mats[0]) - 2 * mean_no_diag(loss_mats[1]) + mean_no_diag(loss_mats[2])
        return mmd2


class GMMMixedLoss:
    """
    Joint objective: NLL + lambda * MMD^2.
    loss_scalers controls the trade-off (e.g., {'nll': 0.01, 'mmd': 1}).
    """
    def __init__(self, loss_scalers: Optional[Dict] = None, **kwargs):
        if loss_scalers is None:
            loss_scalers = {"nll": 0.01, "mmd": 1.0}
        else:
            assert set(loss_scalers.keys()) == {"nll", "mmd"}
        self.loss_scalers = loss_scalers
        self.nll = gmm_nll_dummy_x
        self.mmd = GMMKernelLoss(**kwargs)

    def __call__(self, x, y, preds, use_dist=False):
        return self.loss_scalers["nll"] * self.nll(x, y, preds, use_dist=use_dist) + \
               self.loss_scalers["mmd"] * self.mmd(x, y, preds, use_dist=use_dist)


class ClassificationKernelLoss:
    """
    Classification analog of MMD/IPM that analytically marginalizes predictive randomness under q_theta over classes.
    Computes product-kernel MMD over operands (typically x and y), where k_y is defined on the class-one-hot simplex.
    Complexity O(B^2 C^2) where B is batch size, C is number of classes.
    """
    def __init__(
        self,
        operands: Dict[str, str] = {'x': "rbf", 'y': "rbf"},
        scalers: Optional[Dict] = None,
        bandwidths: Optional[Dict] = {'x': 0.01, 'y': 1.0},
    ):
        assert all([op in VALID_OPERANDS for op in operands.keys()])
        if scalers is None:
            scalers = {op: 1. for op in operands.keys()}
        else:
            assert all(op in scalers for op in operands.keys())
        self.kernel_fun = {op: kernel_funs[k] for op, k in operands.items()}
        self.operands = list(operands.keys())
        self.scalers = scalers
        self.bandwidths = bandwidths

    def __call__(self, x, y, logits, verbose=False):
        loss_mats = [None, None, None]
        for op in self.operands:
            scaler = self.scalers[op]
            bandwidth = self.bandwidths[op]
            if op == 'x':
                assert x.dim() == 2
                loss_mat = loss_mat2 = loss_mat3 = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](x, x, bandwidth)
            elif op == 'y':
                # Expected kernels under q_theta: E_{y_i,y_j~q}[k_Y(y_i,y_j)] etc.
                num_classes = logits.shape[-1]
                y_all = torch.eye(num_classes).to(logits.device)
                k_yy = self.kernel_fun[op](y_all, y_all, bandwidth)   # C x C

                q_y = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)                       # B x C
                q_yy = torch.einsum('ic,jd->ijcd', q_y, q_y)          # B x B x C x C
                total_yy = q_yy * k_yy.unsqueeze(0)                   # B x B x C x C

                k_yj = k_yy[:, y].T                                   # B x C
                total_yj = torch.einsum('ic,jc->ijc', q_y, k_yj)      # B x B x C
                y_one_hot = F.one_hot(y, num_classes=num_classes).float()

                loss_mat  = scaler * total_yy.sum(dim=(2, 3))                    # E[k(y,y)]
                loss_mat2 = scaler * total_yj.sum(-1)                             # E[k(y, y_true)]
                loss_mat3 = scaler * self.kernel_fun[op](y_one_hot, y_one_hot, bandwidth)  # k(y_true,y_true)
            else:
                raise AssertionError(f"Classification supports only operands 'x' and 'y'. Got {op}.")

            for i, value in enumerate([loss_mat, loss_mat2, loss_mat3]):
                loss_mats[i] = value if loss_mats[i] is None else loss_mats[i] * value

        mmd2 = mean_no_diag(loss_mats[0]) - 2 * mean_no_diag(loss_mats[1]) + mean_no_diag(loss_mats[2])
        return mmd2


class ClassificationMixedLoss:
    """Joint objective for classification: NLL + lambda * MMD^2."""
    def __init__(self, loss_scalers: Optional[Dict] = None, **kwargs):
        if loss_scalers is None:
            loss_scalers = {"nll": 0.01, "mmd": 1.0}
        else:
            assert set(loss_scalers.keys()) == {"nll", "mmd"}
        self.loss_scalers = loss_scalers
        self.nll = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
        self.mmd = ClassificationKernelLoss(**kwargs)

    def __call__(self, x, y, logits):
        return self.loss_scalers["nll"] * self.nll(logits, y) + \
               self.loss_scalers["mmd"] * self.mmd(x, y, logits)


# ===== Minimal GMM utilities to create distributions and NLL wrapper =====
# File: src/utils/gmm_utils.py (extracts)
import torch
import torch.distributions as D

def gmm_params_to_dist(mean, std, prob):
    """Return a torch Distribution for a Gaussian (mixture) predictor.
    mean,std,prob: (B, D_y, K)
    For K=1, returns Normal; for K>1, returns MixtureSameFamily(Normal).
    """
    std = std.clip(min=0.05)
    comp = D.Normal(mean.squeeze(-1), std.squeeze(-1))
    assert len(prob.shape) == 3
    if prob.shape[-1] > 1:
        mix = D.Categorical(probs=prob)
        gmm = D.MixtureSameFamily(mix, comp)
        return gmm
    else:
        return comp

# Negative log-likelihood wrapper matching signatures used above
def gmm_nll_dummy_x(x, target, preds, reduce=True, use_dist=False):
    if use_dist:
        dist = preds
        log_prob = dist.log_prob(target).view(-1, 1).mean(dim=-1)
        if reduce:
            log_prob = log_prob.mean(dim=-1)
        return -log_prob
    else:
        mean, std, prob = preds
        dist = gmm_params_to_dist(mean, std, prob)
        log_prob = dist.log_prob(target).mean(dim=-1)
        if reduce:
            log_prob = log_prob.mean(dim=-1)
        return -log_prob


# ===== Example: plug losses into Lightning Modules for training =====
# File: src/models/gmm_module.py (relevant excerpts)
from pytorch_lightning import LightningModule
from src.metrics import GMMNegativeLogLikelihood, GMMCalibrationError

class GMMLitModule(LightningModule):
    def __init__(self, net: torch.nn.Module, criterion, calibrator=None, lr: float = 1e-3, weight_decay: float = 5e-4, kcal_kwargs=None):
        super().__init__()
        self.save_hyperparameters(logger=False)
        self.net = net
        self.criterion = criterion  # e.g., GMMMixedLoss(...)
        self.calibrator = calibrator
        self.train_nll = GMMNegativeLogLikelihood()
        self.val_nll = GMMNegativeLogLikelihood()
        self.test_nll = GMMNegativeLogLikelihood()

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor):
        return self.net(x)

    def step(self, batch):
        x, y = batch
        preds = self.forward(x)              # mean, std, prob
        loss = self.criterion(x, y, preds)   # NLL + lambda * MMD^2 if GMMMixedLoss
        return loss, preds, x, y

    def training_step(self, batch, batch_idx: int):
        loss, preds, inputs, targets = self.step(batch)
        self.log("train/loss", loss, on_step=False, on_epoch=True, prog_bar=True, sync_dist=True)
        self.train_nll(preds, targets)
        return {"loss": loss}

    def configure_optimizers(self):
        return torch.optim.Adam(params=self.parameters(), lr=self.hparams.lr, weight_decay=self.hparams.weight_decay)


# File: src/models/classification_module.py (relevant excerpts)
from pytorch_lightning import LightningModule

class ClassificationLitModule(LightningModule):
    def __init__(self, net: torch.nn.Module, criterion, calibrator=None, lr: float = 1e-3, weight_decay: float = 5e-4, kcal_kwargs=None):
        super().__init__()
        self.save_hyperparameters(logger=False)
        self.net = net
        self.criterion = criterion  # e.g., ClassificationMixedLoss(...)
        self.calibrator = calibrator

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor):
        return self.net(x)

    def step(self, batch):
        x, y = batch
        y = y.squeeze(-1)
        logits = self.forward(x)
        loss = self.criterion(x, y, logits)  # CE + lambda * MMD^2 if ClassificationMixedLoss
        preds = torch.argmax(logits, dim=-1)
        return loss, preds, logits, y

    def training_step(self, batch, batch_idx: int):
        loss, preds, logits, targets = self.step(batch)
        self.log("train/loss", loss, on_step=False, on_epoch=True, prog_bar=True)
        return {"loss": loss}

    def configure_optimizers(self):
        return torch.optim.Adam(params=self.parameters(), lr=self.hparams.lr, weight_decay=self.hparams.weight_decay)
llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
Scope: This extracts and summarizes the IPM/MMD calibration machinery and how to use it as described in the Method.

Key components and how they correspond to the Method:
- IPM/MMD over (Y, Z):
  - Regression: GMMKernelLoss computes an unbiased U-statistic estimate of MMD^2 between the joint distributions of (Y,Z) under truth and forecast, with a product kernel over selected operands (operands subset of {'y','x','p','coords'}).
  - Classification: ClassificationKernelLoss analytically marginalizes predictive randomness over classes in MMD terms, yielding a differentiable O(B^2 C^2) estimator.
- Conditioning variable Z (choose calibration type):
  - Individual/local calibration: operands={'x': 'rbf', 'y': 'rbf'} so k((y,x),(y',x')) = k_Y(y,y') k_X(x,x').
  - Distributional (PIT) calibration or quantile/threshold calibration: include operands={'p':'rbf'} where p = F_theta(y|x) ∈ [0,1]. Optionally multiply with k_X: operands={'p':'rbf','x':'rbf'}
  - Decision-focused Y-kernel: use operands with k_Y as tanh to emphasize threshold/sign decisions: operands={'x':'rbf','y':'tanh'} (and add universal k_Z such as RBF on X for conditional guarantees).
  - Spatial conditioning example: operands can include 'coords' if coordinates are embedded in x.
- Product kernels: The code multiplies per-operand kernel matrices to realize k((y,z),(y',z')) = Π_op k_op(op, op').
- Universal kernels: RBF kernels provided for X, Y, and PIT ensure equivalences where needed; decision-specific kernel tanh is provided for Y.
- Unbiased plug-in U-statistic: mean_no_diag implements the off-diagonal average; MMD^2 = E[k(Z,Z)] - 2E[k(Z,Z')] + E[k(Z',Z')] with off-diagonal means.
- Variance reduction / marginalization:
  - Regression: GMMKernelLoss draws num_samples Monte Carlo rsamples from q_theta(y|x) for Y-kernel terms (default num_samples=10). For mixtures, reparameterized sampling is enforced by prob.shape[-1]==1 for Y; use a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation or use_dist=True with an externally reparameterized distribution if using K>1 components.
  - Classification: ClassificationKernelLoss computes E_q[k_Y(·,·)] analytically via q_y, avoiding sampling.
- Joint training objective: GMMMixedLoss and ClassificationMixedLoss implement min_theta Σ_i -log q_theta(y_i|x_i) + λ·MMD^2. The tradeoff is set via loss_scalers, default {'nll':0.01,'mmd':1.0}. These are plugged into Lightning modules as the training criterion.

Default hyperparameters (can be overridden):
- Regression MMD (GMMKernelLoss):
  - operands: {'x': 'rbf', 'y': 'rbf'} (individual calibration)
  - bandwidths: {'x': 10.0, 'y': 0.1}
  - num_samples: 10
- Classification MMD (ClassificationKernelLoss):
  - operands: {'x': 'rbf', 'y': 'rbf'}
  - bandwidths: {'x': 0.01, 'y': 1.0}
- Mixed objectives:
  - GMMMixedLoss/ClassificationMixedLoss loss_scalers: {'nll': 0.01, 'mmd': 1.0} (λ=1 by default; increase/decrease mmd scale to tune calibration vs. likelihood)

Complexity notes:
- Regression MMD^2 requires forming BxB kernel matrices per operand; when including Y with S predictive samples, it averages over S.
- Classification MMD^2 uses q_yy tensor of shape B x B x C x C; complexity O(B^2 C^2). Reduce B or C if memory constrained.

Usage patterns and examples:
- Individual/local calibration (Z=X) for regression:
  criterion = GMMMixedLoss(loss_scalers={'nll':0.01,'mmd':1.0}, operands={'x':'rbf','y':'rbf'}, bandwidths={'x':10.0,'y':0.1}, num_samples=10)

- Distribution/PIT calibration with optional X-conditioning:
  criterion = GMMMixedLoss(loss_scalers={'nll':0.01,'mmd':1.0}, operands={'p':'rbf','x':'rbf'}, bandwidths={'p':0.1,'x':10.0})

- Decision-focused Y-kernel (threshold/sign) with universal Z-kernel on X:
  criterion = GMMMixedLoss(loss_scalers={'nll':0.01,'mmd':1.0}, operands={'x':'rbf','y':'tanh'}, bandwidths={'x':10.0,'y':1.0})

- Classification with analytic marginalization and joint objective:
  criterion = ClassificationMixedLoss(loss_scalers={'nll':1.0,'mmd':0.5}, operands={'x':'rbf','y':'rbf'}, bandwidths={'x':0.01,'y':1.0})

- Plug into Lightning modules:
  # Regression
  model = GMMLitModule(net=gmm_net, criterion=criterion, lr=1e-3, weight_decay=5e-4)
  # Classification
  model = ClassificationLitModule(net=clf_net, criterion=criterion, lr=1e-3, weight_decay=5e-4)

Forecast distributions and reparameterization:
- gmm_params_to_dist(mean,std,prob) returns a torch Distribution:
  - K=1: Normal with rsample support (reparameterized gradients through Y)
  - K>1: MixtureSameFamily; code asserts single component when sampling Y for MMD, so either:
    - keep K=1 during training when using Y-kernel sampling, or
    - set use_dist=True and construct an external reparameterized sampler (e.g., Gumbel-Softmax for mixture indices, or pass a Differentiable Mixture), or
    - avoid sampling Y by using PIT operand 'p' when applicable.

Batching, normalization, and data:
- Default batch sizes in datamodules: train batch_size=64, test_batch_size=128.
- Datamodules normalize X by default to zero mean/unit var (features with 0 variance set to 0). Consider tuning kernel bandwidths accordingly.

Kernel choices and guarantees:
- Use RBF for universality on continuous spaces (X,Y,U) to obtain conditional calibration guarantees via IPM equivalences.
- For quantile/threshold calibration via PIT, operate over [0,1] with an RBF bandwidth suitable for U: e.g., {'p':0.05-0.2}.
- For decision calibration around a threshold (e.g., sign of Y), use tanh kernel for Y and include a universal kernel on Z (e.g., X) for conditional guarantees.

Metrics and diagnostics (optional):
- GMMKernelCalibrationError and ClassificationKernelCalibrationError provide batch-level MMD^2 estimates as metrics using the same kernels/operands.

Implementation caveats:
- The regression code multiplies kernel matrices across operands to form a product kernel; ensure all operands share the same batch index alignment.
- The unbiased MMD estimator uses off-diagonal means; avoid degenerate batches (B<2).
- For classification, shape is batch-major; ensure labels y are int64, logits float32.
- For large C, memory can spike due to B x B x C x C tensors; adjust batch size or restrict operands to manage memory.

new_method.method:
Motivation
- Operational gap: In energy, finance, logistics, and epidemiology, action is driven by recent performance (e.g., last 12–72 hours) and by streaks of misses. Existing online conformal methods (ACI/PID) only control time-averaged miscoverage and can miss for long stretches after shifts; cross-sectional methods (TQA) assume exchangeability and can yield infinite intervals; offline horizon-joint methods (CopulaCPTS) do not operate in streaming and rely on cross-sectional exchangeability; train-time calibrators (SPCI/MAQR/MMD) do not provide distribution-free online guarantees or adversarial-time robustness. The unmet need is near time-uniform reliability over short windows with explicit safeguards against interval blow-up in a single streaming series.
- Scientific gap: We lack an online, distribution-free controller that (i) targets any interval I (short or long) with provably small deviation of the average error from α, (ii) adapts to unknown drift/regime changes by pivoting to the right temporal scale, and (iii) keeps intervals finite without ad hoc clipping, all while being model-agnostic and lightweight.

Methodology
SAMC2 — Strongly Adaptive Multi-Resolution Conformal Control
Goal: For a streaming series with negatively oriented scores s_t, output C_t = {y: s_t(x_t,y) ≤ q_t} at target miscoverage α, and guarantee for every interval I of length L that |(1/L)∑_{t∈I} err_t − α| ≤ O(√((log K + log T)/L)), while controlling widths and precluding infinite intervals. Distinctives vs prior work: SAMC2 couples (A) multi-resolution controllers, (B) a strongly adaptive sleeping-experts aggregator optimized for both coverage and efficiency, and (C) a dyadic primal–dual feasibility layer that enforces time-uniform constraints using anytime-valid confidence sequences. This differs from PID/ACI (single global integrator, long-run guarantees), TQA (cross-sectional assumptions, occasional infinite PIs), CopulaCPTS (offline joint-horizon, non-streaming), and SPCI/MAQR/MMD (train-time calibration without distribution-free online guarantees).

A) Multi-resolution specialists (per-scale controllers)
- Scales: W_s ∈ {2^0, …, 2^S} with causal kernels w_s(Δ) (exponential λ_s = exp(−1/W_s) or triangular). Expert s is “sleeping” when w_s(0)≈0.
- State per scale s at time t:
  • Error and variance trackers: E_s(t) = ∑_{i≤t} w_s(t−i)(err_i − α); V_s(t) = ∑ w_s(t−i)^2·err_i(1−err_i).
  • I-term (variance-adaptive, anytime-valid integrator): I_s(t+1) = K_I^s · tanh(γ_s · E_s(t)/√(V_s(t)+ε)), with saturation to avoid blow-up.
  • P-term (robust quantile tracking): q_P^s(t+1) = q_P^s(t) + η_s·ψ_δ(err_{t_pred} − α), with Huber ψ_δ and η_s scaled by a winsorized interquantile range of recent scores at scale s.
  • D-term (optional, auto-pruned): short scales use AR/Theta; long scales SARIMA/Prophet. Auto-disable when residual-score ACF at lag 1–3 falls below a threshold to prevent oscillations.
  • Expert proposal: q^s(t+1) = q_P^s + I_s + D_s.
- Implementation delta from PID: kernelized, variance-adaptive multi-scale integrators; per-scale robust learning rates; automatic D-term pruning; experts sleep/awake by kernel support.

B) Strongly adaptive sleeping-experts aggregation with efficiency regularization
- Final threshold: q(t+1) = ∑_s π_s(t)·q^s(t+1).
- Weight updates: Use a second-order, switching-robust learner (AdaNormalHedge or Squint) with sleeping experts. Composite per-expert loss:
  ℓ_s(t) = λ·|err_t − α| + (1−λ)·(width_t^s / Z_t) + β·barrier(q^s(t); q_max(t)),
  where Z_t is a robust scale of recent scores (rolling MAD), width_t^s is the induced interval width for expert s, and barrier(q,q_max)=max(0,((q−q_max)/q_max))^2 with q_max(t) a Catoni/quantile-based 99.5th percentile of scores to preclude infinite intervals.
- Regret guarantee: For any interval I, aggregation loss is within O(√(|I|·log K)+log T) of the best fixed scale on I, enabling rapid pivot after drifts/changepoints.
- Asymmetric tails: run two stacks at α/2 each; share π_s across tails to synchronize scale choices while allowing asymmetric q’s.

C) Dyadic time-uniform constraints via a primal–dual feasibility layer
- Dyadic confidence sequences: Maintain O(log T) anytime-valid CS’s for Bernoulli means on dyadic intervals u using mixture boundaries (e.g., stitching/mixture e-process). Update dual variables:
  μ_u(t+1) ← [μ_u(t) + ρ·(err_t − α)·1{t∈u}]_+, with cap μ_max and variance-adaptive step ρ via empirical-Bernstein scaling.
- Coupling to the aggregator: Modify expert weights via an interval-alignment term
  π_s(t) ∝ exp(−η·∑_{τ≤t} ℓ_s(τ)) · (1 + κ·∑_u μ_u(t)·A_s(u)),
  where A_s(u) is kernel overlap between expert s and dyadic u. This raises “pressure” on scales aligned with intervals exhibiting recent undercoverage, approximating time-uniform constraints online.
- Diagnostics: Produce online p-values for coverage in recent windows from the CS’s, enabling monitoring and alarms without stationarity assumptions.

D) Efficiency/robustness safeguards (finite intervals by design)
- Soft barrier + freeze/relax: If barrier fires for τ consecutive steps, temporarily shrink η_s and enlarge saturation C_sat^s; dyadic duals handle coverage pressure while bounds keep q finite.
- Heavy-tail robustness: Huberized gradients, winsorized ranges, Catoni tails for q_max; asymmetric stacks; optional global shift limited by barrier to avoid set explosion.

E) Multi-horizon and multi-series extensions
- Multi-horizon: replicate specialist stacks per horizon; aggregate within horizon; enforce light cross-horizon coherence via online isotonic projection of widths or a streaming empirical copula regularizer (used as a regularizer only, not for validity).
- Multi-series: hierarchical meta-weights meta-π_s learned across series via online mirror descent; per-series adapters preserve idiosyncrasies.

F) Theory blueprint (proof targets)
- Near time-uniform coverage: With dyadic CS’s and strongly adaptive regret, show that for any interval I of length L, |(1/L)∑_{t∈I} err_t − α| ≤ c·√((log K + log T)/L) + o(1), with c depending on saturation/barrier constants. Contrast: PID/ACI give long-run averages only.
- Efficiency regret: Cumulative width of SAMC2 is within strongly adaptive regret of the best fixed convex combination of specialists that satisfies dyadic constraints.
- Stability: Lipschitz increments in q_t and almost-sure finiteness of q_t due to saturation + barrier even under adversarial streaks; D-term auto-disable ensures no resonant oscillations.

G) Implementation plan (on top of the PID codebase)
- core/mr_controller.py: MultiResController implementing per-scale P/I/D with variance-adaptive I-term and D auto-disable; API: step(score_t, covered_t, meta) → {q^s(t+1)}.
- core/agg_sa.py: Sleeping-experts learner (AdaNormalHedge/Squint), composite loss, asymmetric-tail coupling; API: update({q^s}, err_t, width_t) → q(t+1), {π_s}.
- core/dyadic_cs.py: Anytime CS’s, dual μ updates, alignment A_s(u), p-value endpoints; API: update(err_t) → {μ_u(t)}, influence({π_s}).
- core/guards.py: barrier(q,q_max), Catoni/q̂_max estimators, freeze/relax logic, winsorization.
- core/hier_meta.py: optional hierarchical meta-weights across series.
- Complexity: O(K) per step with K≈S+1 scales and S≈⌈log2 T⌉; memory O(K+log T). In practice, S is small (≤10–12 for most operations windows).

How SAMC2 differs from prior work
- Conformal PID/ACI: Replaces global integrator with multi-resolution controllers, adds strongly adaptive expert aggregation optimizing efficiency under coverage pressure, and enforces time-uniform constraints via dyadic CS’s; prevents infinite intervals without heuristic clipping.
- TQA: Single-series streaming (no cross-sectional exchangeability), strongly adaptive interval-wise control; no infinite PIs; explicit efficiency regularization.
- CopulaCPTS: Online streaming and time-uniform control instead of two-split, offline joint-horizon guarantees relying on cross-sectional exchangeability.
- SPCI/MAQR/MMD: Train-time calibration vs SAMC2’s distribution-free online control with adversarial-time robustness; our method is model-agnostic and wraps any forecaster/score.
- Probabilistic state-space/particle-flow: Orthogonal; SAMC2 is a calibration/control wrapper requiring no generative assumptions.

Expected impact
- Reliability under drift and change points: Near time-uniform coverage in short windows and rapid recovery after shifts, drastically reducing harmful miss streaks in operations.
- Efficiency with guarantees: Tighter, stable, and finite intervals via explicit efficiency regularization and barrier safeguards, with interval-wise regret guarantees.
- Deployability: Lightweight, drop-in streaming layer with O(log T) memory and O(K) time per step, model-agnostic, and equipped with actionable diagnostics (anytime-valid p-values for recent windows).
- Broad applicability: Energy balancing, staffing, routing, and trading—all contexts where short-window reliability is mission-critical—benefit from principled, real-time uncertainty control that prior methods do not provide.

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
Title: Empirical validation plan for SAMC2 — Strongly Adaptive Multi-Resolution Conformal Control

Overview
We propose three complementary experiments that jointly test SAMC2’s core claims: near time-uniform coverage on short windows, fast adaptation after shifts, finite intervals under heavy tails, and efficiency close to the best scale in hindsight. All experiments are implementable in Python with standard packages (numpy, pandas, statsmodels/scikit-learn, numba optional), and minimal custom code for the controller, sleeping-experts aggregator, and dyadic confidence sequences.

Common elements
- Base forecaster (model-agnostic): simple, streaming-capable predictors so that results are attributable to the calibration/controller rather than a complex model.
  • Seasonal-naive + EWMA correction for real data; online AR(1) for synthetic.
- Score: s_t(y) = |y − ŷ_t| (negatively oriented). Prediction interval at time t: C_t = [ŷ_t − q_t, ŷ_t + q_t]. Error err_t = 1{y_t ∉ C_t}.
- Target miscoverage α ∈ {0.1, 0.2}; report α = 0.1 in main.
- SAMC2 configuration (defaults):
  • Scales W_s ∈ {2^0,…,2^9} = {1,…,512}. Exponential kernels w_s(Δ) = exp(−Δ/W_s).
  • I-term: I_s(t+1) = K_I^s tanh(γ_s E_s/√(V_s+ε)), with E_s, V_s per spec. Use γ_s = 1.0. K_I^s = c_I·S_t, c_I=0.25, S_t = rolling MAD of scores (shared across s). ε = 1e−6.
  • P-term: q_P^s(t+1) = q_P^s(t) + η_s·ψ_δ(err_{t_pred} − α). η_s = η0/√(1+W_s), η0 = 0.06·S_t; ψ_δ is Huber with δ=0.5.
  • D-term: off by default; optionally AR(1) on q residuals for s with W_s ≤ 8, auto-disable by ACF threshold 0.2.
  • Aggregation: exp-weights with sleeping experts (feasible approximation to Squint/AdaNormalHedge). Composite loss ℓ_s(t) = λ·|err_t−α| + (1−λ)·(width_t^s/Z_t) + β·barrier(q^s(t); q_max(t)). Set λ=0.7, β=1.0. Z_t = rolling MAD of scores. barrier(q,q_max)=max(0,((q−q_max)/q_max))^2.
  • q_max(t): robust 99.5th percentile of scores via t-digest or Catoni; fallback to 10× median if warm-up insufficient.
  • Dyadic CS layer: maintain dyadic intervals up to length 2^J ≤ T using mixture boundaries (Howard et al. 2021). Update dual μ_u with step ρ scaled by empirical-Bernstein; cap μ_u ≤ 5. Couple to weights with factor κ=0.5.
  • Asymmetric tails: run two stacks at α/2 each; share π_s.
  • Safeguards: if barrier fires τ=5 consecutive steps, halve η_s for active s and double I_s saturation C_sat^s for 50 steps.
- Baselines for all experiments:
  • PID/ACI: single global exponential-integrator controller with the same S_t and barrier as SAMC2 but no multi-scale or CS layer.
  • Rolling Split Conformal (RSC): rolling window W_cal ∈ {128, 512, 2048}; compute empirical (1−α) quantile of past residuals; no barrier (report blow-ups when window too small/heavy tails).
  • Optional: CUSUM-reset ACI: run ACI; when a simple CUSUM test for residual mean/variance fires, reset ACI state and recalibrate on last 512 points.
- Metrics (computed per method):
  • Near time-uniform coverage: for window lengths L ∈ {16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512}, compute sup-window deviation sup_I |(1/L)∑_{t∈I} err_t − α| and average deviation over all sliding windows.
  • Short-window reliability: fraction of sliding windows whose coverage lies within α ± ε (ε ∈ {0.02, 0.05}).
  • Streak risk: distribution of consecutive-miss run lengths; P(run ≥ k) for k ∈ {3,5,7,10}; 95th percentile of run length.
  • Efficiency: average width, 95th percentile width, and cumulative width; width-volatility (std of q_t) and total variation ∑|q_t−q_{t−1}|.
  • Finite-interval safety: rate of barrier activations, rate of width > q_max, and number of infinite/implausible intervals (if any) in baselines.
  • Adaptation: recovery time after a shift to bring 24/48/72-window coverage back within α ± 0.05.
  • CS diagnostics: number of dyadic CS alarms (p ≤ 0.01) per 10k steps; alarm dwell time.
  • Strongly adaptive regret (Exp. 3): on each interval I, Reg_I = ∑_{t∈I} ℓ_SAMC2(t) − min_s ∑_{t∈I} ℓ_s(t).

Experiment 1 — Synthetic drift & heavy-tail stress test
Goal and hypotheses
- H1: SAMC2 achieves smaller short-window coverage deviations and faster recovery after drifts than PID/ACI, RSC, and CUSUM-reset ACI.
- H2: SAMC2 maintains finite, tighter intervals under heavy tails versus baselines without ad hoc clipping.
- H3: Dyadic CS alarms are fewer and shorter under SAMC2.

Data generation (Python-feasible)
- Length T = 50,000. Generate piecewise nonstationary AR signal with changing mean, variance, and tail index; 1% extreme outliers.
- Latent signal: x_t = 0.6 x_{t−1} + u_t; u_t ~ N(0, 0.5^2). Seasonal component: S_t = 2·sin(2π t/24) + 1.5·sin(2π t/168).
- Regimes (deterministic schedule):
  • t∈[1,10k): ε_t ~ t_5(σ=1.0)
  • [10k,20k): mean jump +5; ε_t ~ t_3(σ=1.2)
  • [20k,30k): variance halved; ε_t ~ t_8(σ=0.6)
  • [30k,40k): drift slope +0.01 per step; ε_t ~ mixture 0.9·t_5(σ=1.0)+0.1·N(0,4^2)
  • [40k,50k): abrupt variance doubles; ε_t ~ t_3(σ=2.0)
- Response: y_t = x_t + S_t + ε_t. With prob 0.01, add an outlier +/− 10·S_t sign.
- Base predictor: ŷ_t = 0.9·ŷ_{t−1} + 0.1·(y_{t−1} − season_hat_{t−1}) + season_hat_t, where season_hat_t = y_{t−24} (seasonal naive). Warm-up first 1000 steps.

Methods compared
- SAMC2 (full).
- PID/ACI (single integrator; same S_t and barrier; no CS).
- RSC with W_cal ∈ {128, 512, 2048}.
- CUSUM-reset ACI (variance CUSUM, threshold chosen for ≈1 false alarm per 10k steps in stationary segments).

Protocol
- Run 20 random seeds for outlier signs and u_t; report means and 95% CIs.
- Sliding-window metrics at step 1 for L ∈ {16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512}.
- Track shift times; compute recovery time to re-enter α ± 0.05 for L ∈ {24, 48, 72}.

Acceptance criteria
- Coverage: For L ≤ 128, SAMC2 average deviation ≤ 0.03 and at least 30% lower than best baseline; sup deviation ≤ 0.08 and ≤ best baseline by ≥20%.
- Recovery: Median recovery time after each regime change ≤ 50% of best baseline.
- Efficiency: Average width within 5% of best baseline that meets coverage; 95th width ≤ baseline by ≥10%.
- Finite safety: Zero infinite intervals; barrier activation rate ≤ RSC by ≥50% and ≤ ACI by ≥25%.
- CS alarms: ≤ 0.5 per 10k steps on stationary segments and ≤ 30% lower than baselines overall.

Python implementation sketch (core streaming loop)

# Pseudocode; implementable with numpy/pandas
for t in range(T):
    y_hat = base_predictor.update(y_hist)
    # Scores from past realized residuals used to update SAMC2 state,
    # then produce q_{t+1} to form C_{t+1}. For evaluation, we align so
    # that q_t affects coverage at t (one-step-ahead conformity).
    if t > 0:
        err = int(abs(y_prev - q_center_prev) > q_prev)
        samc2.update_after_observation(err, last_score=abs(y_prev - y_hat_prev))
        pid.update(err, abs(y_prev - y_hat_prev))
        rsc.update(abs(y_prev - y_hat_prev))
        cusum_aci.update(err, abs(y_prev - y_hat_prev))
    # Get next quantile proposals from SAMC2 specialists
    q_s = samc2.controllers_propose()
    q_samc2 = samc2.aggregate(q_s)
    q_pid = pid.propose()
    q_rsc = rsc.propose()
    q_cusum = cusum_aci.propose()
    # Form intervals and evaluate when y_t arrives next iteration
    store(q_samc2, q_pid, q_rsc, q_cusum, y_hat)
    y_prev = y_t; y_hat_prev = y_hat; q_prev = q_samc2; q_center_prev = y_hat

Compute metrics over stored err_t series per method.

Experiment 2 — Real-world operations: electricity load streaming
Goal and hypotheses
- H4: On real, seasonal, drifting data, SAMC2 reduces harmful miss streaks and short-window coverage deviations without inflating intervals.

Dataset
- Electricity Load Diagrams 2011–2014 (UCI). Use total load aggregated across clients, hourly. Alternatively, choose a single zone to avoid cross-sectional dependence. T ≈ 35k hours.
- Split: first 2 weeks for forecaster warm-up; then pure streaming.

Base predictor
- Seasonal naive with weekly seasonality plus EWMA bias correction:
  • season_hat_t = y_{t−168}
  • bias EWMA b_t = 0.98·b_{t−1} + 0.02·(y_{t−1} − y_{t−169})
  • ŷ_t = season_hat_t + b_t

Methods
- SAMC2, PID/ACI, RSC (W_cal ∈ {168, 672, 2688}), CUSUM-reset ACI.

Protocol
- Run once (deterministic), and also add 10 bootstrap resamples via block bootstrap (block size 168) to form uncertainty bands for metrics.
- Evaluate L ∈ {24, 48, 72, 168} for operations windows.

Key metrics
- Coverage deviation over windows; streak metrics (P(run ≥ k), k ∈ {3,5,7}); 95th run length.
- Efficiency: average and 95th width; width volatility.
- Alarms: dyadic CS alarm count and dwell time.

Acceptance criteria
- P(run ≥ 5) reduced by ≥30% vs best baseline; 95th run length reduced by ≥2.
- Window coverage within α ± 0.05 for ≥90% of 24/48/72-hour windows (vs ≤80% for baselines).
- Average width within 10% of the tightest baseline that meets the coverage criterion.

Implementation notes
- Use pandas to load CSV; handle missing values by forward-fill.
- All controllers share the same residual stream |y−ŷ|.
- For RSC, use t-digest or heap to maintain rolling quantiles efficiently.
- For CS layer, compute online Bernoulli CS on err_t and produce alarms; compare across methods (the CS is a diagnostic, not a controller, for baselines).

Experiment 3 — Ablations and strongly adaptive regret
Goal and hypotheses
- H5: Each SAMC2 component contributes measurably; the full system minimizes composite loss close to the best fixed scale on any interval.
- H6: After a change in the optimal temporal scale, SAMC2 pivots quickly (short weight-pivot time) while keeping widths stable.

Synthetic "regime carousel"
- T = 60,000; rotate regimes every 5,000–10,000 steps so that the best scale alternates among W* ∈ {8, 32, 128, 512}:
  • High-frequency drift: y_t = y_{t−1} + ξ_t, ξ_t ~ N(0, 0.5^2)
  • Medium drift + occasional jumps: add jump +A at random times; A ~ Uniform(3,7)
  • Low-frequency drift + heavy tails: ε_t ~ t_3(σ=1.5)
  • Quasi-stationary: ε_t ~ N(0, 0.8^2)
- Base predictor: online AR(1) (recursive least squares) to decouple from simple seasonal naive.

Variants
- Full SAMC2.
- No-CS: SAMC2 with μ_u ≡ 0 (no dyadic layer).
- Uniform-avg: multi-resolution with uniform averaging (no adaptive aggregation).
- Single-scale PID: best-tuned global scale chosen by validation.
- No-efficiency-reg: set λ=1, β=0 in aggregator (coverage-only focus).
- No-barrier: disable barrier (to quantify blow-up risk).

Metrics
- Strongly adaptive regret: For dyadic intervals and for sliding windows of L ∈ {32, 128, 512, 2048}, compute Reg_I = ∑ ℓ_method − min_s ∑ ℓ_s. Plot median Reg_I/√(|I|) across intervals.
- Pivot time: after each regime switch, time until π_{W*} ≥ 0.5.
- Stability: TV(q) and max |Δq_t|; number of oscillation episodes (ACF lag-1 > 0.6 sustained for ≥100 steps in q_t increments).
- Coverage/efficiency as in Experiment 1.

Acceptance criteria
- Full SAMC2 achieves lowest median Reg_I/√(|I|) across L, within 10% of the empirical lower bound given by oracle min_s.
- Pivot time ≤ 1.5× W* on median; significantly faster than uniform-avg and single-scale PID.
- No-barrier variant exhibits ≥5× higher rate of width blow-ups vs full SAMC2; No-CS shows larger short-window deviations.

Key implementation snippets

class MultiResController:
    def __init__(self, scales, alpha):
        ...  # init E_s, V_s, q_P^s, saturation, kernel params
    def update_after_observation(self, err, score):
        for s in scales:
            E_s = lambda_s*E_s + (err - alpha)
            V_s = lambda_s2*V_s + err*(1-err)
            I_s = K_I_s * tanh(gamma_s * E_s / sqrt(V_s + eps))
            q_P_s += eta_s * huber(err - alpha, delta)
            if use_D_term(s):
                D_s = ar1.update(q_resid_s)
            q_s = clip(q_P_s + I_s + D_s, 0, q_max_current * 1.2)
            proposals[s] = q_s
        self.proposals = proposals
    def controllers_propose(self):
        return self.proposals

class SleepingExpertsAggregator:
    def __init__(self, scales, lam, beta):
        self.w = np.ones(len(scales)) / len(scales)
        self.cum_loss = np.zeros(len(scales))
    def update(self, proposals, err, widths, q_max, cs_pressures):
        loss = lam * abs(err - alpha) + (1-lam) * (widths / Z_t) + beta * barrier(proposals, q_max)
        self.cum_loss += loss
        # pressure term from dyadic CS layer (alignment A_s handled externally)
        logits = -eta * self.cum_loss + kappa * cs_pressures
        self.w = softmax(logits)
    def aggregate(self, proposals):
        return float(np.dot(self.w, proposals))

class DyadicCSLayer:
    def __init__(self, T_max):
        self.intervals = build_dyadic_intervals(T_max)
        self.mu = np.zeros(len(self.intervals))
    def update(self, t, err):
        # Maintain mixture e-process or stitching boundaries for Bernoulli mean
        for u in intervals_covering_t(t):
            self.mu[u] = np.clip(self.mu[u] + rho_t * (err - alpha), 0, mu_max)
    def pressures(self, kernels_overlap):
        # Map μ_u to per-scale pressure via overlaps A_s(u)
        return np.dot(kernels_overlap, self.mu)

Evaluation code outline
- Build a common Runner that encapsulates: base_predictor, controllers, metrics collectors.
- For RSC, maintain rolling quantile via heap or tdigest; for PID/ACI, keep single E,V and I-term only.
- Metrics computation uses a streaming buffer or post-run vectorized numpy for sliding windows.

Reproducibility and statistics
- Fix random seeds; log configs; export per-step traces for err_t, q_t, π_s, μ_u.
- Use 20 seeds for Experiment 1 and 3; block bootstrap for Experiment 2.
- Report means with 95% CI via t-interval over seeds/blocks.

Ablation risks and mitigations
- If base forecaster is too weak/strong, it may mask controller differences. We therefore report normalized widths (relative to rolling MAD of residuals) and ensure base predictor is identical across methods.
- CS layer hyperparameters κ and ρ can over-constrain and inflate widths; include a small grid {κ ∈ [0.2, 0.5, 1.0], ρ default/×0.5/×2} checked on a separate synthetic validation stream before final runs.

Expected outcomes summary
- SAMC2 delivers near time-uniform coverage for short windows, with fast recovery after regime shifts and controlled widths, outperforming PID/ACI and RSC. The dyadic CS layer cuts persistent undercoverage episodes without causing blow-ups, and the efficiency-regularized aggregator keeps widths competitive with the best fixed scale on each interval.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
Below is a complete, implementation-ready experimental plan aligned with the Experiment Strategy and the SAMC2 method specification. I follow each item, expand details, consolidate where possible, and provide executable code sketches using PyTorch and widely available libraries (numpy, pandas, statsmodels, tdigest, river, confseq). All experiments are designed to avoid redundant validation and to increase reliability via replication, bootstrapping, and rigorous diagnostics.

Common implementation details shared by all experiments
- Language and libraries
  • Python 3.10+
  • Core: numpy, pandas, scipy, torch (PyTorch), statsmodels, tdigest, river, confseq, numba (optional), scikit-learn (optional), matplotlib/seaborn (for plots)
  • Repro: hydra or argparse + yaml, random seed control in numpy/torch

- Data alignment and one-step-ahead protocol
  • We evaluate one-step-ahead intervals: at time t, we have q_t and ŷ_t; we measure err_t = 1{|y_t − ŷ_t| > q_t}.
  • Updates to controllers use err_t and score_t = |y_t − ŷ_t| immediately after observing y_t; new proposals q_{t+1} are used for step t+1.

- Base forecasters (kept simple and identical across methods to isolate calibration effects)
  • Synthetic: online AR(1) with recursive least squares (river.linear_model.LinearRegression with lag features, or custom RLS) or the simple streaming rule provided in the strategy.
  • Real: seasonal naive with EWMA bias correction; see Experiment 2 snippet.

- Score and prediction sets
  • Score s_t = |y_t − ŷ_t| (negatively oriented). Interval C_t = [ŷ_t − q_t, ŷ_t + q_t]. err_t = 1{y_t ∉ C_t}.
  • Target α ∈ {0.1, 0.2}; α=0.1 reported as main.

- SAMC2 default config (exactly as in the strategy, with operational details)
  • Scales W_s ∈ {1, 2, 4, 8, …, 512}. Exponential kernels w_s(Δ)=exp(−Δ/W_s) implemented via EW updates with decay λ_s=exp(−1/W_s).
  • I-term: I_s(t+1) = K_I^s tanh(γ_s E_s/√(V_s+ε)). E_s and V_s are EW trackers of centered errors and error variance. γ_s=1.0. K_I^s=c_I·S_t, c_I=0.25. S_t=rolling MAD of scores across last 1024 observations (robust scale). ε=1e−6. Saturation C_sat^s clamps |I_s| ≤ 3·S_t (adaptable; doubled under freeze/relax).
  • P-term: q_P^s(t+1) = q_P^s(t) + η_s·ψ_δ(err_t − α). Huber ψ with δ=0.5. η_s = η0/√(1+W_s), η0 = 0.06·S_t. q_P^s initialized to empirical α-quantile from warm-up or median(S_t) if short warm-up.
  • D-term: off by default; if enabled for W_s ≤ 8, use AR(1) on q residuals with statsmodels.tsa.ARIMA or a simple torch-based AR(1); auto-disable when abs(ACF at lags 1–3)<0.2.
  • Aggregation: exp-weights with sleeping experts; composite loss ℓ_s(t)=λ·|err_t−α| + (1−λ)·(width_t^s / Z_t) + β·barrier(q^s(t), q_max(t)). λ=0.7, β=1.0. Z_t=rolling MAD of scores. barrier(q,q_max)=[max(0,(q−q_max)/q_max)]^2.
  • q_max(t): robust 99.5% of scores via tdigest.TDigest; fallback 10× median of scores (warm-up fallback). For α/2 asymmetric stacks compute per-tail caps similarly.
  • Dyadic CS layer: use confseq.bern.cs.Bernoulli anytime-valid confidence sequences on dyadic intervals; maintain dual μ_u with empirical-Bernstein-scaled step ρ_t; cap μ_u ≤ 5. Couple to weights with κ=0.5 via overlaps A_s(u) computed from kernel mass of expert s inside u.
  • Asymmetric tails: two stacks at α/2, share weights π_s. Final q is sum of left/right tail thresholds for symmetric absolute-score intervals.
  • Safeguards: if barrier triggers for τ=5 consecutive steps for expert s, halve η_s and double C_sat^s for the next 50 steps.
  • Complexity: O(K) per step with K≈log2(512)+1≈10.

- Baselines and their implementation choices
  • PID/ACI: single EW integrator with same S_t and barrier; I-term only, no multi-scale or CS. Implement with torch vectors of size 1.
  • Rolling Split Conformal (RSC): maintain rolling empirical (1−α) quantile of past residuals using tdigest for windows W_cal ∈ {128, 512, 2048}. For a strict rolling window, combine a deque of contributions with a pair of t-digests (approximate add/remove) or use river’s RollingQuantile if available; we recommend tdigest with a fixed half-life (EW) as an additional variant for efficiency.
  • CUSUM-reset ACI: implement ACI; detect change via river.drift.ADWIN on squared residuals (variance shifts) or PageHinkley on residuals (mean shifts), thresholds tuned to ~1 false alarm per 10k stationary steps; upon alarm, reset ACI’s internal state and warm-start q from recent MAD.

- Metrics and efficient computation
  • Sliding-window coverage deviation: use torch.nn.functional.conv1d on the 0/1 err vector to get window sums fast.
  • Streaks: compute run-length distribution vectorized by finding indices where err changes; or using numba for speed.
  • Efficiency: average, 95th percentile width; width volatility: std(q_t) and TV(q)=∑|q_t−q_{t−1}|.
  • Finite-safety: barrier activation rate, occurrences of q_t > q_max(t), count “infinite/implausible” intervals for methods not protected (RSC when quantile → ∞ is not literal but we flag q above q_max).
  • Adaptation: recovery time after known shifts to get L-window coverage back within α±0.05, L ∈ {24,48,72}.
  • CS diagnostics: number of CS alarms (p ≤ 0.01) per 10k steps; average dwell time (consecutive steps in alarm state).
  • Strongly adaptive regret (Experiment 3): Reg_I = ∑_{t∈I} ℓ_method(t) − min_s ∑_{t∈I} ℓ_s(t).

- Reproducibility and statistics
  • Fixed seeds per run; log all hyperparameters and library versions; store per-step traces (err_t, q_t, π_s(t), μ_u(t), q_max, barrier flags).
  • Exp 1 and 3: 20 seeds; Exp 2: block bootstrap with block size 168, 10 resamples.
  • Report means and 95% CIs via t-interval across seeds/resamples; add nonparametric bootstrap CIs when helpful.

- Reliability safeguards during implementation
  • Sanity checks: initial calibration on a stationary synthetic stream to verify long-run average coverage ~ α.
  • Stress tests: extreme outlier stress to confirm barrier caps q and freeze/relax logic engages.
  • Hyperparameter sensitivity: small grid over κ ∈ {0.2, 0.5, 1.0}, ρ multipliers ∈ {0.5, 1, 2} on a short validation synthetic stream.

Core PyTorch-oriented code snippets (used across experiments)
1) SAMC2 controllers and aggregator (condensed, ready to expand)

import numpy as np
import torch
from dataclasses import dataclass
from tdigest import TDigest

@dataclass
class SAMC2Config:
    alpha: float = 0.1
    scales: tuple = tuple([2**i for i in range(0, 10)])  # 1..512
    gamma: float = 1.0
    c_I: float = 0.25
    eta0_mult: float = 0.06
    huber_delta: float = 0.5
    barrier_beta: float = 1.0
    lam: float = 0.7
    kappa: float = 0.5
    mu_cap: float = 5.0
    freeze_tau: int = 5
    freeze_len: int = 50
    warmup: int = 1000

class RollingMAD:
    def __init__(self, win=1024):
        self.win = win
        self.buf = []
    def update(self, x):
        self.buf.append(float(x))
        if len(self.buf) > self.win:
            self.buf.pop(0)
    @property
    def value(self):
        if not self.buf:
            return 1.0
        med = np.median(self.buf)
        return max(1e-6, 1.4826 * np.median(np.abs(np.array(self.buf)-med)))

class MultiResController:
    def __init__(self, cfg: SAMC2Config):
        self.cfg = cfg
        S = len(cfg.scales)
        self.device = torch.device('cpu')
        self.scales = torch.tensor(cfg.scales, dtype=torch.float32)
        self.lambda_s = torch.exp(-1.0 / self.scales)
        self.lambda2_s = torch.exp(-2.0 / self.scales)
        self.E = torch.zeros(S)
        self.V = torch.zeros(S)
        self.qP = torch.zeros(S)
        self.I = torch.zeros(S)
        self.D = torch.zeros(S)
        self.freeze_count = torch.zeros(S, dtype=torch.int32)
        self.sat = torch.full((S,), 3.0)  # multiplier of S_t
        self.eta_base = torch.tensor([cfg.eta0_mult/np.sqrt(1.0+w) for w in cfg.scales])
        self.huber_delta = cfg.huber_delta
        self.barrier_fired = torch.zeros(S, dtype=torch.int32)
        self.tdigest = TDigest()  # for q_max
        self.mad = RollingMAD(win=1024)
        self.S_t = 1.0
        self.initialized = False
        self.last_proposals = torch.zeros(S)

    def huber(self, r, delta):
        r = torch.as_tensor(r)
        return torch.where(torch.abs(r) <= delta, r, delta*torch.sign(r))

    def update_stats(self, score):
        self.mad.update(score)
        self.S_t = self.mad.value
        self.tdigest.update(score)

    def q_max(self):
        if self.tdigest.centroids:
            return max(10.0 * 1e-6, self.tdigest.quantile(0.995))
        else:
            # fallback
            return 10.0 * max(1e-6, self.S_t)

    def update_after_observation(self, err, score):
        # Update robust scale trackers
        self.update_stats(score)
        alpha = self.cfg.alpha
        # EW updates
        self.E = self.lambda_s * self.E + (err - alpha)
        self.V = self.lambda2_s * self.V + err * (1 - err)
        # I-term with saturation scaled by S_t
        sat_val = self.sat * self.S_t
        I_raw = self.cfg.c_I * self.S_t * torch.tanh(self.cfg.gamma * self.E / torch.sqrt(self.V + 1e-6))
        self.I = torch.clamp(I_raw, -sat_val, sat_val)
        # P-term
        eta = self.eta_base * self.S_t
        grad = self.huber(err - alpha, self.huber_delta)
        self.qP = torch.relu(self.qP + eta * grad)
        # D-term (off by default; placeholder)
        self.D.zero_()
        # Proposals and barrier
        proposals = torch.relu(self.qP + self.I + self.D)
        qmax = self.q_max()
        barrier = torch.clamp((proposals - qmax) / max(qmax, 1e-6), min=0) ** 2
        fired = (barrier > 0).to(torch.int32)
        self.barrier_fired = fired
        # freeze/relax
        self.freeze_count = torch.where(fired > 0, self.freeze_count + 1, torch.zeros_like(self.freeze_count))
        relax_mask = (self.freeze_count >= self.cfg.freeze_tau)
        self.eta_base = torch.where(relax_mask, self.eta_base * 0.5, self.eta_base)
        self.sat = torch.where(relax_mask, self.sat * 2.0, self.sat)
        self.freeze_count = torch.where(relax_mask, torch.zeros_like(self.freeze_count), self.freeze_count)
        # Final clamped proposals
        proposals = torch.clamp(proposals, 0.0, qmax * 1.2)
        self.last_proposals = proposals
        return proposals.detach().clone(), qmax

class SleepingExpertsAggregator:
    def __init__(self, cfg: SAMC2Config, num_scales: int):
        self.cfg = cfg
        self.S = num_scales
        self.w = torch.full((num_scales,), 1.0/num_scales)
        self.cum_loss = torch.zeros(num_scales)
        self.Z_t = 1.0
        self.eta = 0.5  # exp-weights learning rate; can tune
    def update_scale(self, scores_recent):
        # Update robust Z_t as MAD on recent scores
        if len(scores_recent) > 0:
            med = np.median(scores_recent)
            self.Z_t = max(1e-6, 1.4826 * np.median(np.abs(np.array(scores_recent) - med)))
    def step(self, proposals, err, qmax, cs_pressures=None):
        lam = self.cfg.lam
        beta = self.cfg.barrier_beta
        widths = proposals  # for absolute residual score intervals, width = q
        barrier = torch.clamp((proposals - qmax) / max(qmax, 1e-6), min=0) ** 2
        loss = lam * abs(err - self.cfg.alpha) + (1 - lam) * (widths / max(self.Z_t, 1e-6)) + beta * barrier
        self.cum_loss += loss
        logits = -self.eta * self.cum_loss
        if cs_pressures is not None:
            logits = logits + self.cfg.kappa * cs_pressures
        w = torch.softmax(logits, dim=0)
        self.w = w
        q = float(torch.dot(w, proposals))
        return q, loss.detach().clone()

2) Dyadic CS layer (using confseq)

from confseq import bern_cs

class DyadicCSLayer:
    def __init__(self, alpha, T_hint=100000):
        self.alpha = alpha
        self.t = 0
        # Pre-build dyadic intervals up to log2(T_hint)
        self.levels = int(np.ceil(np.log2(max(2, T_hint))))
        self.intervals = []  # list of (start, end, level)
        for Lpow in range(self.levels+1):
            L = 2**Lpow
            # We won’t enumerate all, but compute on the fly
        self.mu = dict()  # keyed by (start,end)
        self.pressure = None
        self.alarm = False
        # Confseq trackers
        self.cs = bern_cs.BernoulliCS()
        # We also maintain a sliding-window CS summary for diagnostics

    def intervals_covering_t(self, t):
        cov = []
        for Lpow in range(self.levels+1):
            L = 2**Lpow
            start = (t // L) * L
            cov.append((start, start+L-1))
        return cov

    def update(self, t, err, alpha):
        self.t = t
        # Update CS diagnostic for the global stream (optional):
        self.cs.add(err)
        # Update dyadic duals
        cov = self.intervals_covering_t(t)
        for (a,b) in cov:
            key = (a,b)
            if key not in self.mu:
                self.mu[key] = 0.0
            # Empirical-Bernstein step size (simple form):
            # rho_t ~ c / sqrt(length), approximate by 1/sqrt(L)
            L = (b - a + 1)
            rho = 1.0 / np.sqrt(L)
            self.mu[key] = min(5.0, max(0.0, self.mu[key] + rho * (err - alpha)))
        # Alarm: use confseq boundary for running mean at any time (diagnostic)
        lcb, ucb = self.cs.confidence_sequence(confidence=1-0.01)
        self.alarm = (lcb > alpha) or (ucb < alpha)

    def pressures_for_scales(self, t, scales):
        # A_s(u): overlap between exp kernel at scale s and dyadic interval u
        # Approximate by kernel mass centered at t intersecting u.
        press = []
        for s in scales:
            lam = np.exp(-1.0 / s)
            # Overlap weight for each covering interval
            w = 0.0
            for (a,b), mu in self.mu.items():
                # kernel center at t: weight decays backward; overlap approx sum exp(-d/s) over d in [t-b, t-a]
                if a <= t <= b:
                    # recent interval; assign higher overlap
                    L = (b - a + 1)
                    w += mu * min(1.0, L / (L + s))
            press.append(w)
        return torch.tensor(press, dtype=torch.float32)

Note: For rigorous CS per dyadic interval, confseq currently exposes global CS; we use it for diagnostics. The dual updates over dyadic intervals are implemented as a light-weight approximation. If desired, use confseq anytime p-values over batches to refine per-interval alarms.

3) Utility functions for metrics

import torch.nn.functional as F

def sliding_window_mean(x, Ls):
    # x: torch tensor shape (T,)
    x = x.view(1,1,-1)
    means = {}
    for L in Ls:
        k = torch.ones(1,1,L)/L
        s = F.conv1d(x, k, padding=0)
        means[L] = s.view(-1).detach().cpu().numpy()
    return means

def run_length_distribution(err_np):
    # err_np: 0/1 numpy array
    runs = []
    count = 0
    for e in err_np:
        if e == 1:
            count += 1
        else:
            if count > 0:
                runs.append(count)
            count = 0
    if count > 0:
        runs.append(count)
    return np.array(runs) if runs else np.array([0])

4) Baseline implementations (sketch)

class PIDController:
    def __init__(self, alpha, c_I=0.25):
        self.alpha = alpha
        self.E = 0.0
        self.V = 0.0
        self.qP = 0.0
        self.S_t = RollingMAD(1024)
        self.tdigest = TDigest()
        self.lambda1 = np.exp(-1.0/128.0)  # single scale tuned
        self.lambda2 = np.exp(-2.0/128.0)
        self.c_I = c_I
        self.gamma = 1.0
        self.huber_delta = 0.5
        self.eta = 0.06
    def q_max(self):
        if self.tdigest.centroids:
            return self.tdigest.quantile(0.995)
        return 10.0 * max(1e-6, self.S_t.value)
    def update(self, err, score):
        self.S_t.update(score)
        self.tdigest.update(score)
        self.E = self.lambda1 * self.E + (err - self.alpha)
        self.V = self.lambda2 * self.V + err*(1-err)
        I = self.c_I * self.S_t.value * np.tanh(self.gamma * self.E / np.sqrt(self.V + 1e-6))
        grad = np.clip(err - self.alpha, -self.huber_delta, self.huber_delta)
        self.qP = max(0.0, self.qP + self.eta * self.S_t.value * grad)
        q = max(0.0, min(self.qP + I, 1.2 * self.q_max()))
        return q

from collections import deque

class RSC:
    def __init__(self, alpha, W_cal):
        self.alpha = alpha
        self.W = W_cal
        self.deque = deque(maxlen=W_cal)
        self.tdigest = TDigest()
        self.ready = False
    def update(self, score):
        if len(self.deque) == self.W:
            # tdigest doesn’t support deletions exactly; keep deque for exact window quantile offline.
            # For streaming approx, we rebuild tdigest every few steps or maintain two tdigests.
            pass
        self.deque.append(score)
        if len(self.deque) >= max(32, int(0.2*self.W)):
            self.ready = True
    def propose(self):
        if not self.ready:
            return np.median(self.deque) if len(self.deque)>0 else 1.0
        q = np.quantile(np.array(self.deque), 1 - self.alpha)
        return float(q)

from river import drift

class CUSUMResetACI(PIDController):
    def __init__(self, alpha, c_I=0.25):
        super().__init__(alpha, c_I)
        self.adwin = drift.ADWIN()
    def update(self, err, score):
        # Detect change on squared residuals
